raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

Poor? Yes. Defeated? Not yet.
If there's one thing I've learned, it's that you must never back down, and never give up.
Take a moment to reflect on this:
"The reason you have to follow your dreams is because even your dreams are trying to get away from you."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 2 ~ ~ ~  True
Best night's sleep in a long time. You even folded your blanket. Who are you?
Tonight is brutally cold. You can see your breath inside the car.
You curl into a ball on the back seat, pulling your jacket over your head.
Every exhale fogs the windows. Your hands go numb. Your toes go numb.
Morning can't come soon enough.
You step out of the car and into the cool night air. Your joints pop. All of them. In sequence. Like a xylophone of decay.
You walk along the shoulder of the highway. Headlights sweep over you like searchlights. Nobody stops. Nobody cares.
It's nice, actually. Being nobody. Being invisible. Just a silhouette on the roadside that exists for two seconds in someone's headlights and then doesn't.
When you get back to your car, you feel calmer. Tired in the right way.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You've got that look in your eye. Hope. Or madness. Sometimes they're the same.

Your neck doesn't hurt. Your back doesn't hurt. Nothing hurts. Is this a trap?
You find an old photo in your glove box. You forgot it was there.
It's you. From before. Smiling. Happy. With people who loved you.
You stare at it for a long time. Who was that person? Where did they go? Are they in here somewhere, buried under the gambling and the car-sleeping and the existential dread?
Sitting in your car, your mind drifts. You dream of water. Dark water. Deep water. Rising water.
You're in a car—your car—and water is pouring in through every crack.
You can't open the doors. The pressure is too much. The windows won't break.
The water reaches your waist. Your chest. Your neck. Your mouth.
You scream but water fills your lungs instead of air.
You wake up GASPING. Clawing at your throat. Soaking wet with sweat.
Just a dream. Just a dream. Just a—
Your feet are wet. You look down.
Rain. It's raining. Water leaked through a crack in your window.
Just rain. You're fine. You're fine. You're fine.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Come, sit down, we have a game to play.

with matching black hat and boots, and a short brown beard.
He's riding a magnificent horse, muscular, but nimble, each step powerful, but precise.
The man reaches your window, and in a deep southern accent, he begins to talk to you.
"Howdy, partner! The name's Jameson. Davey Jameson.
"I happen to notice you were admiring my steed. He's a beauty, isn't he. "
"You see, it's common courtesy when a cowboy rides by, "
to give their mighty steed a carrot, as a way to express your gratitude for their hardwork and commitment to the job."
"You my friend, you are carrotless. That's quite a disrespectful showing towards my steed. "
"My, my, this can't do at all. What if another cowboy comes by, you're just gonna disrespect their steed, too? "
"Tell you what, I happen to have one spare carrot in my pouch. Take this, and be ready. "
"You never know when a cowboy's gonna come trotting by."
Davey Jameson hands you his Carrot, and smiles.
"See, with this carrot in your possession, you're ready for anytime a cowboy strolls on down this road. "
"Just give their steed a carrot, and they'll be very grateful."
And with that, Jameson reins his horse high into the air, gives you a yee-haw, then dashes off down the road.
You do some stretches. Yoga-ish movements. Look like an idiot in a parking lot.
But you know what? You feel better. Less like a rusty robot.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The night is young. Your wallet, however...

This quote always gets me going:
"I honest to God thought Santa Claus was real for the longest time. Mom and Dad just never told me. My parents are fucking cruel."

You wake up and actually want to get out of the car. This is a historic moment.
Through your car window, you spot a rat watching you from a storm drain. It seems... curious.

Best night's sleep in a long time. You even folded your blanket. Who are you?
You drive your car to a gas station for supplies. Just snacks. Maybe some coffee.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
Best night's sleep in a long time. You even folded your blanket. Who are you?
You drive your car to a gas station for supplies. Just snacks. Maybe some coffee.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You duck behind a shelf. Slowly, carefully, you crawl toward the back.
The robber is focused on the register. You're almost to the storage room...
You make it. Hide behind boxes of toilet paper and canned goods.
You can hear everything. The shouting. The crying. The crash of the register hitting the floor.
Then sirens. Running footsteps. Silence.
The police find you an hour later, still hiding. Curled up like a child.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.
For once, you sleep peacefully. No dreams. No nightmares. Just rest.
You wake up feeling... good? Is this what normal people feel like?
It won't last, but you appreciate it while it does.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You smell like daylight and poor decisions. Sit.

Here's a quote my dad used to say:

"Let's embark on a journey, a meandering path not unlike the serpentine wanderings of a leaf caught in a capricious autumn breeze, which, as it dances to the silent music of nature, reminds us of the unpredictable choreography of existence. This leaf, let's call it Gerald, flutters with an elegance borne of happenstance, a fragile vessel for the whims of the wind. Gerald's journey is not linear, nor is it bound by the rigid expectations of society's ceaselessly grinding gears. Instead, Gerald twirls, dips, and soars, embracing the chaos with a grace we can only aspire to. Now, consider the ant, industrious and steadfast, a creature of purpose and communal toil. Our ant, whom we'll name Beatrice, marches diligently on her quest for sustenance, her life a testament to the virtues of hard work and persistence. Yet, in the grand tapestry of the cosmos, what is Beatrice but a speck, a mere blip on the infinite canvas of the universe? And yet, does her insignificance in the face of the vast unknown render her efforts moot? I posit that it does not, for in the grand scheme, all actions, large and small, contribute to the intricate mosaic of existence. But back to Gerald, who by now has traversed the convoluted landscapes of our imagination, touching upon the existential questions that haunt the periphery of our consciousness. What can we learn from Gerald and Beatrice? Is it their resilience, their unyielding will to persist in the face of the insurmountable odds stacked against them by the very nature of their existence? Or is it, perhaps, the simple beauty of their dance, a reminder that life, in all its complexity and confusion, offers moments of sublime beauty, fleeting and precious, to be cherished and remembered? As Gerald finally comes to rest upon the earth, joining Beatrice in the eternal cycle of life and death, we are reminded that all journeys, whether they be of leaves or ants or the human heart, are interconnected in the grand, bewildering dance of the cosmos. So, what was the point of this story, you may ask? Well, that, my friend, is entirely up to you."

The mime stares at you. You stare back.

Then he mimes... crying? Counting money? Losing at cards?
Wait. Is he acting out YOUR life?
The mime finishes with a dramatic death scene, tongue out and everything, then springs back up and takes a bow.

As you walk along the path, you notice someone leaning against a tree in front of you.
As you get closer, you notice that the person's face is blue, their eyes are bloodshot, and they don't appear to be breathing.
You begin to panic, before thinking through the situation.
They're already dead, so there's nothing you can do to help them.
Maybe they had some money on them? I mean, they're not gonna use it. Why shouldn't you?
Do you search the body?
You rummage through the pockets, trying to find anything worthwhile.

Inside the wallet, you find $148 dollars.

You slept like a baby. A big, grown baby in a car, but a baby nonetheless.
Something triggers it. A sound. A smell. Suddenly you're THERE again.
The casino. The Dealer. The losses. The fear. You relive it all in an instant.
When you come back to reality, you're curled on the floor, shaking.
PTSD episode. The trauma is embedded in your nervous system.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you drive over to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.

You park your wagon right up front next to the entrance, and step out towards the doors.
When you enter into the lobby, you're immediately hit with the strong smell of hand sanitizer in the air.
The carpets are dull and brown, the light above you is flickering,
and the walls are filled with posters telling you to 'Floss More Often!' and 'Wash Your Hands Before You Eat!'
If you didn't know any better, you would have guessed you were on a movie set.
Walking towards the front desk, you see a cheery old lady, who looks up from her computer to smile at you.
Her gray hair covers her glasses, and her hand trembles as she hands you a pen and a clipboard with some paperwork.
Of course it's paperwork.
After filling out your information, you walk back to the front desk, and hand the lady the clipboard. She smiles, and begins to speak to you.
I see you're here for a checkup. The Doctor will see you now.
Hey there champ! How are you? Doing all right? Let's check you out and make sure you're all up to snuff.
What you're experiencing - the flashbacks, the hypervigilance - it's PTSD. Trauma therapy, possibly EMDR, medication to help manage symptoms.
Well, that seems to be everything. You still appear a little worse for wear, but this medicine should do the trick.
You walk back to the front desk to checkout.

Your body feels like it got a factory reset overnight. Joints? Smooth. Back? Fine. Mood? Immaculate.
You feel anxious.
The cards in your dreams are always face down. Always.
You wake up to scratching noises. Something is ON your car.
You look up. A family of raccoons is making themselves at home on your roof.
Mom raccoon. Dad raccoon. Three baby raccoons. All staring at you through the windshield with those little bandit masks.
You decide to let them have this one. You go back to sleep.
In the morning, they're gone. So is your windshield wiper. So is the antenna. And half a sandwich you forgot about.
Raccoons. Nature's tiny, adorable home invaders.

You get in your car and drive to Tom's Trusty Trucks and Tires.
"Well howdy there, stranger! Name's Tom. Welcome to Tom's Trusty Trucks and Tires!"
The old mechanic wipes his hands on a rag that's seen better days.
His eyes are kind, but tired. The kind of tired that comes from years of hard work and harder choices.
"You got the look of a man runnin' from somethin'. I seen it before. Hell, I been there myself."
He gestures to a faded photograph on the wall. A younger Tom, standing with a woman and two small children.
"Family's a funny thing, yunno? You don't realize what you got 'til it's gone. "
"Spent years chasin' money, chasin' dreams. Almost lost everything that mattered."
He taps the photo frame with a calloused finger.
"But it ain't never too late to go back. That's what I learned. It ain't never too late to pick up the phone and say sorry."
He looks at you with something like recognition.
"Anyway. What can I do for ya today?"

"You look like a collector. A finder of rare things."
He glances around nervously, then leans closer.
"Name's Gus. Grimy Gus, they call me. Got a little shop down on Fifth and Nowhere. "
"If you ever find yourself with... unusual items... things that don't belong in the light of day... I can make them disappear. For a fair price."
He taps his nose conspiratorially.
"Collectibles, trinkets, treasures. Things you picked up on your... adventures. I don't ask questions. Just cash on the barrel."
He hands you a grimy business card through the crack in the window. It reads: "Grimy Gus's Pawn Emporium - We Buy What Others Won't"
"Come by sometime. You won't regret it. Probably."
He shuffles off into the morning mist before you can respond.

your eyes pop open and you're ready to go. did someone slip you a good dream? because that was chef's kiss.
a glossy black limousine is quietly approaching your wagon.
as you sit up from your slumber, you read 'oswald's optimal outoparts' cursively engraved in gold letters on the side of the limo.
waving the vehicle down, the limo slows, then stops before you.
the door opens vertically, and a large red carpet is rolled out onto the street.
you watch in awe as a man, with a combover and a tuxedo, walks out before you. he coughs, then speaks.
"why hello there! the name's oswald, as you can see by my nametag.
do you like my bowtie? well of course you do! it appears your limousine has broken down."
oswald pulls a gold whistle out of his pocket, and blows into it deeply.
"oh stuart!" you watch as a bald man in a tailcoat suit, no taller than 4 feet, hobbles over to oswald's side.
"this is stuart! he will fix your limousine up for a fair price.
let's say, i don't know, i suppose a fair price is 500,000 dollars.
okay, the look on your face says that i'm making a big mistake.
let's try $900, and stuart here will get you back on the road! do you accept?"
"really? you don't want my services? i'm so sorry stuart, but it appears they don't want our services."
stuart begins to break down into tears, and he runs quickly back into the limo.
"shame on you! shame on you! i hope to never see the likes of you again."
you watch as oswald rolls up the red carpet, gets back in the limo, and drives off into the distance.
something's definitely wrong with your body. that's for certain.

Do you sit and listen?

After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself at the edge of a swamp.
The air is thick and humid, smelling of rot and growing things.
Cypress trees rise from the murky water, draped in Spanish moss.
You hear music. Faint, plucking strings. A banjo? Out here?
You find an old man on a stump, playing a battered instrument. No teeth. Clothes more patches than fabric.
He stops when he sees you. "Don't get many visitors out here."
Do you sit and listen?

raw_recent_text: As you fall asleep, you dream of sitting at a blackjack table.
The familiar green felt stretches out before you, and across from you sits the Dealer,
shuffling cards with practiced ease.
You're dealt a hand. A King and an Ace. Blackjack.
But instead of paying out, the Dealer's face contorts with rage. He slams his fist on the table.
"You think you can just WIN?! You think it's that EASY?!"
His yelling gets louder and louder. At first he's yelling at himself, muttering about odds and luck. But then, he turns to you. His eyes bore into yours.
"This is YOUR fault. ALL OF IT."
The screaming grows deafening until you jolt awake, heart pounding.
The Dealer's rage echoes in your mind long after you wake.

You're walking to the casino when a black sedan pulls up beside you.
The window rolls down. Tony's face appears. He's not smiling.
"Boss says you've been avoiding him. That's not smart."
Before you can respond, he's out of the car. His fist connects with your stomach.
You double over. He hits you again. And again.
"That's a reminder. Next time, we take something you can't grow back."

raw_recent_text: As my doctor was putting me under, he whispered into my ear:
"Whisper to the rain and listen to the wind, for they tell tales of square watermelons and the secret life of shadows who are afraid of the dark."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 34 ~ ~ ~  True
Your sanity: BROKEN
Your reflection in the cards is smiling. You're not.
A bird is singing outside your car. You don't want to punch it. Progress.
You've been fighting sleep for days. Coffee. Slapping yourself. Driving with the windows down.
It doesn't matter. It comes when it wants to.
This time, you don't even realize you've fallen asleep. One moment you're driving.
The next, you're parked somewhere you don't recognize, engine off, and you can't move.
The shadow fills the entire car. It's everywhere. In the mirrors, in the corners,
in the gaps between the seats.
"I'm not what you think I am."
"I'm not a monster. I'm a door. Walk through me and the pain stops. The cold stops. The hunger, the loneliness, the endless, stupid cards — all of it. Gone."
"I've been watching you lose for so long. I'm offering you the only guaranteed win."
The cold is so intense your teeth are chattering. Or trying to — your jaw is locked.
Inside your head, you hear the question clearly:
"Do you accept?"
